english: W. for p'yed, p'yen (phyed, phyen); p'ed-p'ed (phed phed) v. p'yad-p'yad (phyad phyad).

1. pf. p'ebs (phebs), resp. to go C.; to come C. and W.; also čág (cag) (or p'yág- (phyag-)) p'eb-pa (pheb pa); scarcely in ancient lit., but Glr., Pth., Mil.; *nyi-ráṅ-la p'éb-loṅ yód-na* (nyi rang la pheb long yod na) W., if you have time to come; *°o-ná ghá-le p'eb* ('o na ga le pheb) C., well, good bye! *dha sá-hib p'eb* (da sa hib pheb), id. in speaking to a European; čag p'eb źu ṅaṅ (cag pheb zhu ngang) v. snaṅ-ba (snang ba) I. extr.; p'éb-par smrá-ba (pheb par smra ba) Sch., to salute; Sch. also: to speak politely (??); p'éb-par p'ág-pa (pheb par phag pa), Sch., to rise gracefully, to walk decently (?); p'éb-sgo ltar (pheb sgo ltar) Sch.: 'according to the given order', but cf. gro-sgo ltar (gro sgo ltar) under sgo (sgo) 3; p'éb-rdog-pa (pheb rdog pa) 'to tramp arrival', to go to welcome a high Lama or other honoured person on his arrival with dance and music C., Lexx.; Cs. however mentions p'eg-rdób (pheg rdob) as a musical instrument, 'a small brazen plate for music', and in Stg. the same word occurs along with sil-bsnyán (sil bsnyan). — 2. for °bab ('bab); so it seems to be used, Lt.: tur-du mi p'ebs (tur du mi phebs); p'ó-bar mi p'ebs (pho bar mi phebs), it won't go down his throat.
to be able Mil. nt., cf. also dod (dod); Cs.: 'to become, to be fit' etc.

I. num. figure: 134. II. man, opp. to woman, male, p'o lo lṅá-bču-pa (pho lo lnga bcu pa), men of the age of fifty (opp. to bu-mo lo-gnyís-ma (bu mo lo gnyis ma)) Ma.; p'o mčór-po (pho mchor po), a handsome man (opp. to bud-méd mčór-mo (bud med mchor mo)) Pth.; as a pleonastic apposition to the pers. pron., like mi (mi), Mil.; common in C.: *p'o-ṅá* (pho nga), I (masc.) = k'ó-bo (kho bo); esp. in reference to animals: male, he (ass), cock (bird), Dzl. and elsewh.; as apposition to the names of domestic animals when castrated: p'o-rtá (pho rta), gelding; rá-p'o (ra pho), a castrated he-goat. — p'o-skyés (pho skyes), man, male person, Pth. — p'o-gós (pho gos), man's dress, man's coat; p'o-čás (pho chas), Mil. id. (?) — p'o-čéṅ (pho cheng) Wts., Sch., gelding. — p'o-tó (pho to) Bal., stallion. — p'o-rtágs (pho rtags) 1. Physiol. = p'o-mtsán (pho mtshan). 2. Gram.: sign for the masculine gender, Cs. — p'o-nád (pho nad), 1. W. andromany, inordinate desire after men. 2. v. p'ó-ba (pho ba). — p'o-mó (pho mo), man and woman, men and women, male and female; p'o-mó med (pho mo med), no difference of sex exists. *p'o-tsé* (pho tse) Bal., male sex. — p'o-mtsan (pho mtshan), membrum virile, man's yard, esp. the penis; the rather vague expression p'o-mtsán (pho mtshan) (or p'o-rtágs (pho rtags)) bčád-pa (bcad pa) is asserted to apply not to castration (Schr.), but only to circumcision (which, however, is not generally known in Tibet, Mussulmans being found only in some of the larger cities of the country). — p'o-yan (pho yan) Sch. and p'o-raṅ (pho rang) Cs., p'o-hraṅ (pho hrang) C., an unmarried man. — *p'o-ri* (pho ri) W., *p'o-ré* (pho re) C. a male kid. — p'o-lhá (pho lha), 1. tutelary deity of a man's right side (ni f.) Glr. 2. Cs.: Sir, as polite address. — (Observation: The circumstance of the consonants of the alphabet and the prefix-letters being divided by Tibetan grammarians into masculine, feminine and neuter, is of no practical moment: careful investigations on that head have been made by Schiefner and Lepsius). III. v. p'ó-so (pho so).
Sch. (perh. p'o-gyó (pho gyo) v. gyo-mo (gyo mo)), hollow tile.

less frq. p'ó-nya-ba (pho nya ba) (Ssk. दूत), 1. messenger, e.g. sent for a physician; p'ó-nya gtóṅ-ba (pho nya gtong ba), p'ó-nya mṅág-pa (pho nya mngag pa), to send, dispatch a messenger; brtsí-ba (brtsi ba), Cs. to receive one (?) — 2. ambassador, envoy. — 3. Passages like gśin-rǰei p'ó-nya (gshin rje'i pho nya) messenger of death, and bdé-ba-čan-gyi p'ó-nya (bde ba can gyi pho nya), honourable epithet of a king, that is looked upon as a demi-god (similarly to ἄγγελος τοῦ παραδείσου) sufficiently justify the application of the word to the scriptural notion of angel, which may be rendered still more intelligible by adding nám-mk'ai (nam mkha'i), Chr. P. (P. Georgi retains the Italian angelo, spelling it ản-bye-lo ('an bye lo)). Buddhist mythology has no available type for it, and lha (lha) (Cs.) could only be made use of, if already whole generations of the Tibetan nation had become Christians.
(resp. sku-lto (sku lto) Cs.) 1. stomach — 2. the second cavity of the stomach or reticulum of ruminating animals (cf. gród-pa (grod pa)). p'ó-ba ltód-pa (pho ba ltod pa), Cs. to overcharge the
